A rebalance is triggered when group membership changes (a consumer joins, leaves, or is considered dead after missing heartbeats / exceeding max.poll.interval), or when the subscribed partitions change. The group coordinator reassigns partitions to members, and under the classic eager protocol consumption pauses while assignments are recomputed. Frequent rebalances therefore cause repeated processing stalls; this is why cooperative/incremental rebalancing and tuned session/poll timeouts exist.
explanations.a: Wrong. Membership changes (not just adding a topic) trigger rebalances, and a rebalance does not reset committed offsets to earliest; consumers resume from their last committed offsets.
explanations.c: Brokers do not fire rebalances on a fixed timer, and messages are not 'permanently lost' by a rebalance. Offsets are committed, so consumption resumes where it left off (possibly reprocessing uncommitted messages).
explanations.d: Commits do not trigger rebalances. Duplicate delivery can occur around a rebalance for uncommitted work, but a rebalance is not caused by committing, nor does it duplicate every committed message.

question: A payment service consumes from an at-least-once stream and must charge each order once. Engineers want 'exactly-once' end-to-end results at the application boundary. Which approach actually delivers effectively-once processing for the external side effect (the charge)?

choices.text: Rely on the network being reliable enough that duplicates effectively never happen, so no dedup is needed.
Attach a unique idempotency/dedup key to each message and have the consumer record processed keys atomically with the side effect, so a redelivered duplicate is recognized and skipped.
Switch the consumer to commit offsets before processing each message, so a message is never seen twice.
Increase the broker replication factor so messages are never redelivered to the consumer.

explanations.b: Correct. True end-to-end exactly-once across an external side effect is achieved through deduplication on a stable key: the consumer checks/records a unique message (or business) key in the same atomic operation (e.g. a transaction or conditional write) that performs the charge. On redelivery the key is already present, so the charge is skipped. This makes processing effectively-once even though delivery remains at-least-once.
explanations.a: Wrong and dangerous. Distributed messaging produces duplicates from retries, rebalances, and crashes regardless of network quality; assuming they 'never happen' guarantees occasional double charges.
explanations.c: Committing offsets before processing converts the problem into at-most-once: if the consumer crashes after committing but before charging, the message is lost (no charge) rather than duplicated. It does not give exactly-once.
explanations.d: Replication factor affects durability and broker fault tolerance, not consumer-side redelivery. Consumers still reprocess uncommitted messages after a crash or rebalance, so replication alone never prevents duplicates.

question: A consumer keeps failing to process one malformed message ('poison pill'). Without a dead-letter queue (DLQ), what failure mode is most likely, and how does a DLQ address it?

choices.text: Without a DLQ the consumer silently drops the message; a DLQ instead forces the broker to retry it forever until it succeeds.

Without a DLQ the consumer can get stuck retrying the same un-processable message and block the partition behind it; a DLQ lets the consumer route the message aside after N attempts so the rest of the stream keeps flowing.
Without a DLQ the message is delivered to every consumer group at once; a DLQ ensures only one group receives it.
Without a DLQ offsets cannot be committed at all; a DLQ exists purely to store committed offsets for failed messages.

explanations.b: Correct. With ordered partition consumption, a message that always fails (a poison pill) can stall progress: the consumer cannot commit past it without either losing it or looping on it, blocking everything behind it in that partition. A DLQ breaks the deadlock by moving the message to a separate queue/topic after a bounded number of retries, so the consumer advances and the bad message can be inspected or reprocessed out of band.
explanations.a: Wrong on both halves. The default failure mode is usually getting stuck or dropping per the consumer's error handling, not a guaranteed silent drop, and a DLQ does not mean infinite retry; it is precisely the escape hatch from infinite retry.
explanations.c: Fan-out to multiple consumer groups is independent of DLQs; a DLQ does not control how many groups receive a message.
explanations.d: Offsets can be committed without any DLQ. A DLQ is a destination for un-processable messages, not a store of offsets.

question: A team needs all events for a given user processed in order. They use a Kafka topic with multiple partitions. Which producer choice preserves per-user ordering, and what is the inherent limitation of that guarantee?

choices.text: Kafka guarantees total ordering across the whole topic automatically, so any partitioning preserves per-user order with no limitation.

Key messages by user id so all of a user's events hash to the same partition; ordering is then guaranteed only within each partition, not across partitions, and is tied to that user mapping to one partition.
Use a random partitioner for even load; ordering is preserved because each consumer sorts messages by timestamp before processing.
Set the producer to round-robin across partitions and rely on the consumer group to reassemble global order after the fact.

explanations.b: Correct. Kafka guarantees ordering only within a single partition. Producing with the user id as the message key makes the default partitioner hash all of that user's events to the same partition, preserving their relative order. The inherent limits: there is no ordering guarantee across partitions (so different users may interleave arbitrarily), and all of a hot user's traffic lands on one partition, which can create a hotspot and caps that user's throughput.
explanations.a: Wrong. Kafka does not provide total topic-wide ordering across partitions; ordering is a per-partition property only. Relying on automatic global order is the core misconception here.
explanations.c: A random partitioner scatters a user's events across partitions, destroying per-user order. Consumer-side timestamp sorting cannot reliably reconstruct order because messages arrive on independent partitions with no bounded skew and timestamps may collide or be non-monotonic.
explanations.d: Round-robin spreads a user's events across partitions, and consumer groups do not reassemble a global order; each consumer independently reads its assigned partitions, so cross-partition order is lost.

question: A Kafka topic stores the latest profile state per user keyed by user id, with log compaction enabled (instead of time/size retention). What does compaction guarantee, and what is a consequence consumers must handle?

choices.text: Compaction deletes all messages older than the retention period; consumers must re-fetch from the origin database for anything older.

Compaction retains at least the latest value for each key while reclaiming superseded older values; a consumer reading the whole log sees the current state per key, but intermediate historical updates for a key may be gone.
Compaction merges all partitions into one so that ordering becomes global; consumers must therefore re-key their data.
Compaction compresses message payloads with gzip to save space; consumers must decompress each record before use.

explanations.b: Correct. Log compaction keeps, for each key, at least the most recent value, while the background cleaner removes earlier records that have been superseded by a later value for the same key. This lets the topic act as a durable 'latest state per key' changelog (e.g. for rebuilding state on startup). The consequence: a consumer can no longer assume every historical update for a key is present, so it must be designed to reconstruct state from latest-per-key values rather than replaying a complete update history. (Deletes are represented by tombstone records.)
explanations.a: That describes time/size-based retention, the alternative to compaction. Compaction is keyed and retains the latest value per key rather than dropping everything past a time threshold.
explanations.c: Compaction operates per partition and per key; it does not merge partitions or change ordering semantics into a global order.
explanations.d: Payload compression (gzip/snappy/etc.) is a separate, codec-level feature. Log compaction is about removing superseded keyed records, not byte-level compression.

question: A team wants to stream every row change from their primary OLTP database into Kafka for downstream consumers, without modifying application write paths. Which Change Data Capture (CDC) approach is most robust, and why?

choices.text: Poll each table every few seconds with SELECT ... WHERE updated_at > last_seen; it captures all changes reliably with no missed updates or deletes.
Tail the database's write-ahead/transaction log (e.g. via Debezium) and publish the decoded change events; it captures inserts, updates, and deletes in commit order without touching application code.
Have the application dual-write each change to the database and to Kafka in the same request, guaranteeing the two stay consistent.
Take a full table snapshot every hour and diff it against the previous snapshot to derive changes in real time.

explanations.b: Correct. Log-based CDC reads the database's write-ahead/replication log (the same stream replicas consume) and converts committed changes into events. It captures every insert, update, and delete in commit order, imposes minimal load, requires no application changes, and does not miss hard deletes; tools like Debezium implement exactly this.
explanations.a: Query/timestamp polling is fragile: it misses hard deletes (a deleted row no longer matches the query), can miss rows updated within the same timestamp tick, adds query load, and has latency bounded by the poll interval. It is a fallback, not the robust option.
explanations.c: Dual writes do not guarantee consistency: the process can crash between the DB commit and the Kafka publish, leaving them divergent. This is the classic dual-write problem that CDC (or the transactional outbox pattern) exists to avoid, and it also requires modifying write paths.
explanations.d: Hourly snapshot diffing is high-overhead, far from real time, and scales poorly on large tables. It also struggles to attribute ordering to individual changes within the interval.

question: When designing a feed/notification fan-out, what is the core trade-off between fan-out-on-write (push) and fan-out-on-read (pull)?

choices.text: Push precomputes and writes each event into every recipient's feed at publish time (fast reads, expensive writes for high-follower producers); pull assembles the feed by querying sources at read time (cheap writes, more expensive reads).
Push and pull are identical in cost; the only difference is push uses TCP and pull uses HTTP.
Push makes reads slower because each reader must contact every source, while pull makes writes slower because every write is duplicated.
Pull guarantees real-time delivery while push always introduces unbounded delay, so push is never used for feeds.

explanations.a: Correct. Fan-out-on-write (push) does the work at publish time by inserting the item into each follower's materialized feed, so reads are cheap but a celebrity with millions of followers makes a single write enormously expensive. Fan-out-on-read (pull) leaves the work to read time by querying the followed sources and merging, so writes are cheap but reads are heavier. Real systems often hybridize: push for normal accounts, pull for very high-follower producers.
explanations.b: Wrong. The difference is where the fan-out work happens (write time vs read time) and its cost profile, not the transport protocol.
explanations.c: Backwards. Push makes reads fast (precomputed feed) and writes expensive; pull makes reads expensive (query at read time) and writes cheap.
explanations.d: Both are used in practice. Push is in fact common precisely because it gives fast reads; it does not inherently introduce unbounded delay.

question: A streaming consumer cannot keep up with producer throughput and its in-memory buffer is growing without bound, risking out-of-memory crashes. Which response constitutes genuine backpressure (rather than a workaround that just defers the failure)?

choices.text: Keep enlarging the consumer's buffer (and the broker's retention) so it can hold more in-flight messages.

Propagate the slowness upstream: the consumer pulls at its own sustainable rate (or signals the producer to slow), so the queue/log absorbs the lag and the producer is throttled rather than overwhelming the consumer.
Silently drop the oldest buffered messages whenever the buffer is near full so it never overflows.
Spin up unlimited consumer threads on the same machine so more messages are processed concurrently.

explanations.b: Correct. Backpressure is the mechanism by which a slow downstream stage regulates the rate of upstream stages so the system runs at the slowest component's sustainable throughput. In a pull-based log like Kafka this is natural: the consumer fetches only what it can handle and the unconsumed data sits durably in the log as lag, throttling effective end-to-end rate without unbounded buffering. In push systems it is implemented via credit/flow-control signals that tell the producer to slow down.
explanations.a: Enlarging buffers only postpones the overflow; an unbounded or perpetually growing buffer converts a rate mismatch into a delayed crash and adds latency. It is the opposite of applying backpressure.
explanations.c: Dropping data may be an acceptable load-shedding policy for some workloads, but it is data loss, not backpressure; it sacrifices completeness rather than regulating the producer's rate.
explanations.d: Adding consumers/threads can raise capacity up to a point (and only up to the partition count for a Kafka group), but unbounded local threads contend for finite CPU/IO and do not regulate the producer; if the real bottleneck is downstream, this still collapses without flow control.

question: A Kafka consumer is configured with enable.auto.commit=true (offsets committed periodically) and does its message processing asynchronously after each poll. After a crash, some already-acknowledged work is repeated. Why, and what delivery semantic does this configuration give?

choices.text: Auto-commit gives exactly-once: the repetition must be a bug, since committed offsets are never reprocessed.

Auto-commit can advance the offset on a timer for messages that have been polled but whose async processing has not finished; a crash then reprocesses everything after the last committed offset, yielding at-least-once delivery.
Auto-commit commits the offset only after processing fully completes, so a crash can only lose messages, giving at-most-once delivery.
Auto-commit disables offset tracking entirely, so the consumer always restarts from the beginning of the topic.

explanations.b: Correct. Periodic auto-commit advances committed offsets based on what has been polled/returned, on a timer, independent of whether your asynchronous processing has actually finished. On restart the consumer resumes from the last committed offset, so any messages whose processing was in flight or not yet durably effected are reprocessed. The resulting semantic is at-least-once (duplicates possible), which is why idempotent handling or manual commit-after-processing is recommended.
explanations.a: Wrong. Auto-commit does not provide exactly-once; reprocessing after a crash is the expected behavior, not a bug.
explanations.c: That would describe committing only after processing completes (manual commit), and even then a crash between processing and commit causes reprocessing, not loss. Periodic auto-commit specifically does not wait for your async processing to finish.
explanations.d: Auto-commit still tracks and persists offsets to the __consumer_offsets topic; it does not restart from the beginning except when there is no committed offset and auto.offset.reset is set to earliest.

question: A monolithic API server is CPU-saturated at peak. The team debates moving from a single large machine to a fleet of identical smaller machines behind a load balancer. Which statement most accurately captures the defining advantage of horizontal scaling (scale-out) over vertical scaling (scale-up)?

choices.text: Horizontal scaling raises a single node's ceiling indefinitely, so you never have to change the application architecture.

Horizontal scaling adds more nodes so capacity is not bounded by the largest available machine and a single node failure removes only a fraction of capacity, but it requires the workload to be distributable across nodes.
Horizontal scaling is always cheaper per unit of work than vertical scaling because commodity machines cost less than large ones.
Horizontal scaling eliminates the need for a load balancer because clients pick a node at random.

explanations.a: Backwards: indefinitely raising a single node's ceiling describes vertical scaling, which is exactly what hits a hardware limit. And distributing work across many nodes usually does force architectural concerns (statelessness, shared state, coordination), so the claim of no architecture change is wrong.
explanations.b: Correct. Scale-out grows capacity by adding commodity nodes, so you are not capped by the biggest single machine, and losing one node degrades rather than kills the service. The catch is that the workload must be partitionable/parallelizable across nodes, which is why statelessness and load distribution matter.
explanations.c: Tempting but not a guarantee. Horizontal scaling adds coordination, networking, and operational overhead; per-unit cost depends on the workload. Vertical scaling can be cheaper and simpler up to a point. Cost is a trade-off, not the defining advantage.
explanations.d: Wrong. Spreading traffic across many nodes is precisely why you need a load balancer (or equivalent routing). Random client-side selection is one strategy but horizontal scaling does not remove the need to distribute requests.

question: A web tier keeps each user's login session in the local memory of whichever node served the first request, so the load balancer is configured with sticky sessions (session affinity) to keep returning a user to the same node. The team now wants to autoscale this tier aggressively. Why does in-memory session state plus sticky sessions undermine smooth horizontal scaling, and what is the standard fix?

choices.text: Sticky sessions overload the load balancer's CPU; the fix is to add a second load balancer in front of the first.

Because session state lives on one node, removing that node (e.g. during scale-in or a crash) logs those users out and load is unevenly pinned to existing nodes; the fix is to make services stateless by externalizing session state to a shared store so any node can serve any request.
Sticky sessions force every request through the database, so the fix is to add a read replica.
Sticky sessions break TLS termination, so the fix is to move TLS to the application nodes.

explanations.a: Sticky-session routing is cheap for the load balancer and is not a CPU bottleneck. Stacking load balancers does not address the real problem, which is node-local state being lost when nodes change.
explanations.b: Correct. With state pinned to a node, scale-in or a node failure destroys those sessions and new nodes cannot pick up affinitied users, so scaling is neither smooth nor even. The canonical remedy is to make the tier stateless by moving session data to a shared external store (e.g. Redis or a signed token), so the load balancer can route any request to any node and nodes can be added or removed freely.
explanations.c: Sticky sessions route a user to the same app node; they do not force every request to the database. Adding a read replica addresses read scaling, not the lost-state-on-node-change problem.
explanations.d: Session affinity and TLS termination are independent concerns. Sticky sessions do not break TLS, and relocating TLS termination does nothing about the in-memory session state being tied to a single node.

question: A rate limiter must enforce a global per-API-key limit across a fleet of 20 stateless gateway nodes behind a load balancer. Each node initially enforces the limit using only its own in-process counter. Clients routinely exceed the intended global limit. What is the core problem, and what is the standard way to enforce a correct global limit?

choices.text: Per-node local counters each allow the full limit, so the effective global limit is up to N times too high; the fix is to track counts in a shared centralized store (e.g. Redis) that all nodes read and increment atomically, accepting some added latency and a dependency on that store.
The load balancer is using round-robin instead of least-connections; switching the algorithm makes the local counters correct.
The limit is too low; raising each node's local limit to the global value makes the aggregate correct.
Local counters are correct as long as clocks are synchronized with NTP; the only fix needed is tighter time synchronization across nodes.

explanations.a: Correct. If every one of N nodes independently permits the full limit, the fleet collectively allows up to N times the intended rate, and uneven routing makes it worse. Enforcing a true global limit requires shared state: a centralized counter (commonly Redis with atomic INCR/Lua or a token-bucket script) that all nodes consult, trading a little latency and a dependency on that store for correctness. Sloppier-but-cheaper variants divide the budget per node or sync counters periodically.
explanations.b: The LB algorithm changes how requests spread but does not make N independent local counters add up to one global limit. Even perfectly even routing still lets each node permit its own full share, overshooting the global cap.
explanations.c: This makes it strictly worse: raising each local limit to the global value means each node alone allows the entire global budget, so the aggregate balloons to N times the limit.
explanations.d: Clock sync helps align time windows but does nothing about the fundamental issue that counts are not shared. Twenty perfectly-synchronized independent counters still sum to far more than one global limit.

question: During an unexpected traffic spike, a service's queues back up, latency climbs, and it begins to crash under load, taking down even the requests it could have served. The team adds load shedding. What does load shedding do, and why is it preferable to letting the overloaded service absorb everything?

choices.text: It buffers all excess requests in a large in-memory queue so none are dropped and every client is eventually served.

It proactively rejects or degrades a portion of incoming requests once the service nears its capacity limit, so the requests it does accept are served within SLO instead of the whole service collapsing and serving nothing well.
It automatically provisions more servers so the spike is absorbed without rejecting any traffic.
It retries failed requests more aggressively so transient overload is smoothed out.

explanations.a: Unbounded buffering is the failure mode load shedding prevents. Queueing everything during sustained overload just grows latency without bound and eventually exhausts memory; the service still falls over. Shedding deliberately drops work rather than queueing it forever.
explanations.b: Correct. Load shedding sacrifices some requests on purpose (rejecting with a fast 429/503 or serving a degraded response) once the system is near capacity, so the accepted subset stays within latency targets. A partial, predictable degradation beats a total collapse where retries and queue buildup cause even serveable requests to fail.
explanations.c: That describes autoscaling, which is complementary but slower to react (boot time, signal lag) and bounded by limits/cost. During a sudden spike you still need shedding to protect the service until capacity catches up.
explanations.d: Aggressive retries during overload make things worse, adding more load to an already saturated service (a retry storm). Load shedding is about rejecting work, the opposite of piling on retries.

question: A service calls three downstream dependencies (A, B, C) from a single shared pool of worker threads. When dependency C becomes slow, all threads end up blocked waiting on C, and calls to the healthy A and B also start timing out. Which resilience pattern directly addresses this, and how?

choices.text: The circuit breaker pattern, by retrying C until it recovers.
The bulkhead pattern, by isolating resources (e.g. separate thread pools or connection pools per dependency) so a slowdown in C exhausts only C's pool and leaves capacity for A and B.
The retry-with-backoff pattern, by spacing out the calls to C until threads free up.
The cache-aside pattern, by serving C's responses from cache so threads never block.

explanations.a: A circuit breaker (stopping calls to C after failures) is complementary and would help here, but the specific problem described is resource exhaustion of a shared pool, and retrying C is the opposite of what a breaker does. The pattern named for isolating that shared resource is the bulkhead.
explanations.b: Correct. The bulkhead pattern partitions resources so one dependency cannot consume them all, just as a ship's bulkheads keep one flooded compartment from sinking the vessel. Giving each dependency its own thread/connection pool means C's slowness saturates only C's pool, preserving capacity to keep serving A and B.
explanations.c: Backoff controls retry timing; it does nothing about the shared pool being fully occupied by in-flight slow calls to C. The threads are blocked on outstanding requests, not on retry scheduling.
explanations.d: Caching C's responses might reduce calls to C, but it does not solve the general problem of one slow dependency monopolizing a shared resource pool, and it is inapplicable when responses are not cacheable. Resource isolation is the bulkhead, not caching.

question: After a downstream service briefly fails, thousands of clients all retry. Even with exponential backoff, the clients keep retrying in synchronized waves (at ~1s, ~2s, ~4s, ...), repeatedly hammering the recovering service at the same instants. What is the standard refinement, and why does it help?

choices.text: Switch from exponential to fixed-interval retries so the load is constant and predictable.
Add randomized jitter to the backoff delays so clients' retries spread out over time instead of synchronizing into thundering-herd spikes that repeatedly overwhelm the recovering service.
Remove backoff entirely and retry immediately, since the service has more capacity right after recovery.
Increase the maximum retry count so eventually enough attempts succeed.

explanations.a: Fixed intervals make synchronization worse, not better: all clients that failed together would now retry together at every identical tick, producing periodic spikes. The problem is correlation in timing, which a constant interval does not break.
explanations.b: Correct. Exponential backoff alone still leaves retries phase-aligned, so adding jitter (randomizing each delay, e.g. full or decorrelated jitter) decorrelates the clients and smears retry traffic across the interval. This flattens the thundering-herd spikes and gives the recovering service a steadier, survivable load.
explanations.c: Immediate retries with no backoff are exactly the thundering herd that overwhelms a fragile, just-recovered service, turning a brief outage into a sustained one. This is the opposite of the fix.
explanations.d: More retries increases total load and prolongs the herd; it does not address the synchronized timing that causes the repeated spikes. The fix is spreading retries out, not attempting more of them.

question: A team autoscales a latency-sensitive request/response service purely on average CPU utilization with a target of 70%. The service spends most of its time waiting on a slow downstream call (I/O-bound), so CPU stays low even as request latency and queue depth climb, and the autoscaler never adds capacity. What is the lesson about choosing autoscaling signals?

choices.text: CPU is always the correct autoscaling signal; the target should simply be lowered to 30%.

The scaling signal should reflect the actual bottleneck and user-facing health (e.g. request concurrency/queue depth, latency, or requests-per-instance), because a single resource metric like CPU can miss saturation when the workload is bound by something else such as I/O.
Scaling on request latency directly is the only valid approach and CPU should never be used.
The service should be scaled manually because autoscaling cannot work for I/O-bound workloads.

explanations.a: Lowering the CPU target does not help when CPU is not the bottleneck at all; the service can be overloaded at the queue/downstream level while CPU stays flat. A lower threshold would still ignore the real saturation signal.
explanations.b: Correct. The right autoscaling signal must track where the system actually saturates and what users experience. For an I/O-bound service, in-flight concurrency, queue depth, requests-per-instance, or latency reflect load far better than CPU. The general lesson: pick a signal aligned with the bottleneck and user-facing SLOs, not a default resource metric.
explanations.c: Overcorrection. Latency can be a good scaling signal, but it is noisy and lagging, and CPU is perfectly appropriate for CPU-bound workloads. The point is matching the signal to the bottleneck, not banning CPU outright.
explanations.d: Autoscaling works fine for I/O-bound services when driven by an appropriate signal (e.g. concurrency or requests-per-instance). Falling back to manual scaling is unnecessary and discards the benefit of automation.

question: A dashboard shows the service's average (mean) latency is a healthy 40 ms, yet users complain about slowness and the p99 latency is 1,200 ms. Why is p99 often a better SLO target than the average, especially for user-facing services?

choices.text: The average is computed incorrectly; a correctly computed mean would equal the p99.

The average hides the tail of slow requests, while p99 (the value 99% of requests are faster than) exposes the worst experiences that real users hit, which matter because a single page often makes many backend calls and is gated by its slowest one.
p99 is just the average plus a fixed safety margin, so it always tracks the mean.
The average is better; p99 should be ignored because only 1% of requests exceed it and those users do not matter.

explanations.a: The mean is not miscomputed here; a low mean with a high p99 is exactly what a right-skewed latency distribution looks like. The mean and a high percentile legitimately differ; one is not an error of the other.
explanations.b: Correct. Latency distributions are typically long-tailed, so a few very slow requests barely move the mean but define the p99. Tail latency matters because a single user action often fans out into many backend calls and is bounded by the slowest, so a 1% per-call tail can affect a large fraction of page loads. Tracking p99 (and p999) surfaces the experience the average conceals.
explanations.c: p99 is a distribution percentile, not a fixed offset from the mean. In a skewed distribution it can be many multiples of the average, and the gap varies with the shape, so it does not track the mean by a constant margin.
explanations.d: This dismisses the worst-served users and ignores fan-out amplification: with many calls per request, a 1% tail surfaces far more often than 1% of page loads. Tail latency is a primary driver of perceived slowness, which is why teams set SLOs on high percentiles.

question: You must estimate the steady-state server count for a service expected to handle 60 million requests per day, where each request needs about 50 ms of CPU time and each server has 4 usable cores. Using a back-of-the-envelope approach (and ignoring peak-vs-average bursting for a first pass), which estimate and method is right?

choices.text: 60M/day is ~694 requests/sec on average; at 50 ms each that is ~34.7 core-seconds of work per second, so ~35 cores, i.e. about 9 servers of 4 cores for the average load (then add headroom for peaks and redundancy).
60M requests/day divided by 4 cores = 15M, so you need 15 million servers.
Because each request takes 50 ms, one server handles 20 requests/sec, so 60M/day needs exactly one server.
Latency per request does not affect server count; only the daily request total matters, so size purely on storage for 60M records.

explanations.a: Correct. Convert to a rate: 60,000,000 / 86,400 s ~= 694 req/s. Each request consumes 50 ms = 0.05 core-seconds of CPU, so demand is 694 * 0.05 ~= 34.7 core-seconds per second, i.e. ~35 busy cores. At 4 cores/server that is ~9 servers to cover average load, after which you add headroom for peak-to-average ratio, target utilization, and redundancy. This rate-times-cost-per-request method is the standard capacity estimate.
explanations.b: Dimensionally meaningless: dividing a per-day count by cores-per-server yields neither a rate nor a server count. It ignores that requests are spread over 86,400 seconds and that each consumes only a fraction of a core-second.
explanations.c: It conflates per-core throughput with per-server throughput and forgets the rate. One core does ~20 req/s at 50 ms each, and a 4-core server ~80 req/s, but average demand is ~694 req/s, so one server is far short.
explanations.d: Per-request CPU cost is central to compute sizing; the daily total alone cannot tell you how many cores you need. Storage sizing is a separate dimension and does not determine the server count for serving request load.

question: A company scales its stateless web tier horizontally to dozens of nodes, but all of them talk to a single primary database that is now the throughput bottleneck and a single point of failure. Which characterization and next step is most accurate?

diagram.alt: Many stateless web nodes all funnel into one shared primary database, illustrating that the stateless tier scales out while the single database remains the bottleneck and single point of failure.
diagram.source:
graph TD
  C[Clients] --> LB[Load Balancer]
  LB --> W1[Web node 1]
  LB --> W2[Web node 2]
  LB --> W3[Web node 3]
  W1 --> DB[(Single primary DB)]
  W2 --> DB
  W3 --> DB

choices.text: Adding more web nodes will keep improving throughput indefinitely, since the tier is stateless.

Scaling a stateless tier only moves the bottleneck to the next shared resource; the database now caps throughput and is a single point of failure, so the next steps target it (read replicas, caching, sharding/partitioning, and a failover replica for availability).
The fix is to make the web tier stateful so it can answer without the database.
The single database is fine because stateless web nodes never overload a database.

explanations.a: False. Once the web tier is no longer the constraint, piling on more web nodes just sends more concurrent load to the shared database and yields no further throughput, a classic serial-bottleneck limit. Statelessness scales the tier, not the shared dependency behind it.
explanations.b: Correct. Horizontal scaling relocates the bottleneck to whatever resource is still shared, here the single primary DB, which now bounds throughput and is also a single point of failure. The appropriate next moves act on that resource: offload reads with replicas and caching, partition/shard writes to spread load, and add a standby/failover replica to remove the SPOF.
explanations.c: Making the web tier stateful would sacrifice the easy horizontal scaling you just achieved and reintroduce node-affinity and lost-state problems, without fixing the underlying need for a durable shared datastore. The data still has to live somewhere consistent.
explanations.d: Stateless web nodes absolutely can overload a database; in fact scaling them out increases concurrent connections and queries to the single primary, which is precisely why it became the bottleneck and the SPOF.
